notes.anchor: there are many kinds of letters, but one beyond all doubt, for the sake of which the thing itself was invented
notes.note: Cicero is invoking a commonplace of ancient epistolary theory: the letter as surrogate conversation between absent friends. He will proceed to enumerate the genres, showing self-conscious awareness of the letter as a literary form.

notes.anchor: By Hercules, I do not think any citizen can laugh in these times.
notes.note: Written in 53 BC, a year of severe political crisis: gang violence disrupted the elections, Rome was without consuls for months, and the political atmosphere under the Triumvirate was oppressive. Cicero's remark reflects genuine civic anxiety.

notes.anchor: I neither dare write what I think, nor wish to write what I do not think.
notes.note: A pointed admission that Cicero censors his political views in letters, which could be intercepted; the republic's deterioration made frank written opinion dangerous, and letters were read by slaves, carriers, and political enemies alike.

The Latin exspectatio — the public anticipation of a young man's performance on entering political life — was a real social force in Rome; a highly anticipated debut that disappointed could permanently damage a reputation.
